coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of biomass-derived carbon dots as an efficient corrosion inhibitor
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Li, Xue-Qi
Yu, Yang
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Metallic corrosion threatens the safety and reliability of metallic facilities and equipment, and exacerbates the issues of environmental pollution and economic losses. However, it is still a challenge to obtain green inhibitors employing sustainably renewable and economical materials. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are prepared via green hydrothermal treatment only using lichi leaves as the raw material, and their corrosion inhibition properties for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl are systematically investigated using weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurement for the first time. Specifically, the as-obtained eco-friendly lichi leaves-derived CDs (LCDs) possess numerous O- and N-containing functional groups. And these groups endow LCDs almost unchanged intensities of photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) peaks for two weeks in 1 M HCl solution, demonstrating a long-term dispersion stability of LCDs. On the premise, LCDs at only 200 mg/L concentration reveal an eminent corrosion inhibition ability with a high inhibition efficiency of 98.06% for Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl. Meanwhile, they enable the surface roughness (S a) value of steel surface to starkly reduce by 73.7% (from 12.428 to 3.264 μm) by comparison with that of blank HCl solution. Furthermore, based on analyses of adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology, their inhibition mechanism is attributed to the protective film formed by LCDs chemical and physical adsorptions. This work firstly substantiates the remarkable inhibition capacities of biomass-derived CDs and provides their green synthesis approach, beneficial to stimulate the advancement of green and efficient corrosion inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inhibitor
Green synthesis
Inhibition mechanism

Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Marine Civil Engineering

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Project of Shenzhen, China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundations of China, NSFC-Shandong Joint Fund (No. U2006223), the Science and Technology Project of Shenzhen, China (No. JCYJ20190808151011502), the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Marine Civil Engineering (SZU) (No. 2020B1212060074) and the Guangdong University Student Science and Technology Innovation Cultivation Special Fund Project (No. pdjh2021b0428). Furthermore, the authors would also like to thank Tang Xiang Gang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for SEM and EDS measurement. In addition, the authors thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University for the assistance with analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (with a size distribution, inset) and (b) HRTEM images (with a crystal lattice of (100) facet, inset) of LCDs; (c) AFM image and (d) the height profile along the blue line in the AFM image of LCDs.
(a) FTIR of LCDs; (b-d) XPS survey, C1s, O1s and N1s spectra of LCDs.

(a) PL spectra and (b) UV–vis spectra of LCDs with various contents in 1 M HCl; (c) PL spectra and (d) UV–vis spectra of LCDs at the concentrations of 25 and 50 mg/L before and after two-week standing.

Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl including different contents of LCDs: (a) Inhibition efficiency of LCDs under various immersion time; Electrochemical characterizations of (b) Bode plot, (c) Nyquist plot and (d) PDP.
SEM images and EDS spectra of Q235 carbon steel samples in different states: (a) original state, and states after 96 h immersion in 1 M HCl (b) without and (c) with 200 mg/L LCDs.
Inhibition mechanism of LCDs for Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution.
The fitting parameters of Q235 carbon steel in various solutions.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of biomass-derived carbon dots as an efficient corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, Shenzhen Durability Center for Civil and Transportation Engineering, Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, Guangdong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering
Shenzhen Durability Center for Civil and Transportation Engineering
Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure
College of Civil and Transportation Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, Shenzhen Durability Center for Civil and Transportation Engineering, Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metallic corrosion threatens the safety and reliability of metallic facilities and equipment, and exacerbates the issues of environmental pollution and economic losses. However, it is still a challenge to obtain green inhibitors employing sustainably renewable and economical materials. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are prepared via green hydrothermal treatment only using lichi leaves as the raw material, and their corrosion inhibition properties for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl are systematically investigated using weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurement for the first time. Specifically, the as-obtained eco-friendly lichi leaves-derived CDs (LCDs) possess numerous O- and N-containing functional groups. And these groups endow LCDs almost unchanged intensities of photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) peaks for two weeks in 1 M HCl solution, demonstrating a long-term dispersion stability of LCDs. On the premise, LCDs at only 200 mg/L concentration reveal an eminent corrosion inhibition ability with a high inhibition efficiency of 98.06% for Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl. Meanwhile, they enable the surface roughness (S a) value of steel surface to starkly reduce by 73.7% (from 12.428 to 3.264 μm) by comparison with that of blank HCl solution. Furthermore, based on analyses of adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology, their inhibition mechanism is attributed to the protective film formed by LCDs chemical and physical adsorptions. This work firstly substantiates the remarkable inhibition capacities of biomass-derived CDs and provides their green synthesis approach, beneficial to stimulate the advancement of green and efficient corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic corrosion is one of the most concerned topics for human development, not only critically threatening the safety and reliability of metallic facilities and equipment, but also dramatically exacerbating the issues of environmental pollution and economic losses. It is estimated that the direct metallic corrosion cost has been more than $4 trillion per year globally [1]. Various methods involving anticorrosive coating, cathodic protection and corrosion inhibitor have been developed for metal corrosion protection. And the corrosion inhibitor is regarded as the most significant approach to efficiently mitigate the metallic corrosion rate, benefiting from its strong adaptability, simple operation and economic efficiency [2]. Traditional inorganic inhibitors including chromate and mercury salts as well as phosphorus-containing compounds demonstrate favorable inhibition performance, but they fail to meet the demand of environmental protection owing of hazardous effects. To overcome the shortcoming, multitudes of organic compounds such as triazoles [3], imidazoles [4], Schiff bases [5], oral medicine [6], amino acids [7–9], ionic liquids [10–12] and plant extracts [13–15] have been developed as corrosion inhibitors. Among them, amino acids, ionic liquids and plant extracts considered as green corrosion inhibitors have attracted increasing interest. For example, plant extracts can be obtained using sustainable and low-cost raw materials such as plant seeds [15], leaves [14] and flowers [13]; Amino acids and ionic liquids possess the attribute of environmental friendliness. Nevertheless, they still suffer from some unavoidable faults. For example, plant extracts are generally subject to tedious procedure, and employing toxic solvents is necessary [13–15], adversely affecting the surroundings; While ionic liquids and amino acids share the demerit of high cost. The above cases restrict their potential applications. Therefore, elaborately developing a simple and eco-friendly approach to prepare a novel and efficient green corrosion inhibitor is urgently essential.

Fortunately, due to facile synthesis, easily available precursors, tunable surface groups, excellent dispersibility and benign environmental friendliness, carbon dots (CDs), as a new zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial discovered in 2004 [16], have been employed as a novel and green corrosion inhibitor, and displayed remarkable corrosion inhibition properties. For example, Cui, Qiang, Zhang, Zhu et al. have employed antibiotic aminosalicylic acid [17,18], p-phenylenediamine [19] and o-phenylenediamine [20,21] to prepare CDs with high corrosion inhibition performance for carbon steel and copper. And Chen’s group, Ye’s group and Yadav’s group have synthesized N-doped CDs and N, S-codoped CDs as excellent corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel or aluminium using thiourea [22,23], n-butylamine [24], methacrylic acid [25], urotropine [26,27] pyromelletic acid [28] or malononitrile [29] as precursors. Up to now, a range of small organic molecule as starting materials have been adopted to form CDs, and these CDs have exhibited outstanding corrosion inhibition efficiency of more than 90% for various metals involving carbon steel, copper and aluminium in acid and chloride corrosive solution. As a result, CDs are expected to a promising candidate for the next generation green corrosion inhibitor.

Despite the tremendous progress in anti-corrosion field of CDs, none of CDs in above literatures are recognized as true green inhibitors since several obvious drawbacks are unavoidable: (1) unrenewable and high-cost raw materials. Compared with conventional inorganic inhibitors, all the literatures above do not employ heavy metal-containing substances, but a number of organics including benzene structures are extensively applied to form CDs, harmful to operator health and possibly affecting CDs quality. And the whole precursors mentioned above fail to meet eco-friendly, renewable and low-cost criteria. (2) Security concerns and environmental pollution. Although methods in the reports above such as hydrothermal treatment get rid of complicated procedure, poisonous by-products and gases like NO2 are potentially generated on account of toxic precursors. More severely, some precursors such as urotropine [26,27] tend to explode during high-temperature and high-pressure synthesis processes. Taking these considerations into account, using green, easily available, sustainably renewable and economical materials to prepare eco-friendly CDs with high corrosion inhibition properties is indispensable.

Biomass possess advantages of easy acquisition, inexpensiveness, eco-friendliness, abundant sources and renewability, opening up a new way for the preparation of eco-friendly CDs. More importantly, biomass is able to endow CDs surface with rich oxygenic functional groups, conducing to enhance their corrosion inhibition behaviors. Currently, multitudes of researchers have successfully prepared biomass-derived CDs from starch [30], salvia miltiorrhiza [31] and leaves of prunus lannesiana [32], gingko [33], taxus [34] and poplar [35]. And by means of their excellent water solubility, high photostability and low cytotoxicity, biomass-derived CDs have been employed in plant systems [31], bioimaging [35], lubrication [33], sensing [32] and optoelectronics [34]. Conversely, to the best of our knowledge, there is no literature focusing on the application of biomass-derived CDs in the important field of corrosion protection. It can be asserted that natural biomass-derived CDs with efficient corrosion inhibition capabilities acting as true green inhibiters will possess an enormous application prospect. As a result, it is highly desired to realize the green preparation of biomass-derived CDs and investigate their corrosion inhibition performance.

Herein, biomass-derived CDs originating from renewable lichi leaves are prepared by a green, facile and one-step hydrothermal method, avoiding the employment of hazardous elements and the generation of toxic gases, and their corrosion inhibition characteristics are studied in detail by weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) for the first time. Remarkably, the as-obtained lichi leaves-derived CDs (LCDs) with numerous O- and N-containing functional groups demonstrate a long-term dispersion stability in HCl solution. Under this premise, LCDs possess an excellent inhibition rate up to 98.06% at 200 mg/L concentration, thereby acting as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl corrosive solution. In addition, their inhibition mechanism explored by adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology analyses is ascribed to the protective film. This work firstly proves the significant inhibition capacities of biomass-derived CDs, and provides their green synthesis route. These findings would accelerate the development of green and efficient corrosion inhibitors.

Q235 carbon steel with elemental compositions (wt%): C 0.2, Si 0.3, Mn 1.20, Cr 0.25, P 0.045, S 0.05, Cu 0.25, Ni 0.25 and Fe remainder purchased by the local supplier is employed for weight loss and electrochemical studies. Prior to each experiment, Q235 carbon steel specimens are polished using several grades of emery papers ranging from 400 to 2000 grid sequentially, then washed by deionized (DI) water and degreased with ethanol, finally dried under cold air flow. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR grade 36%∼38%) provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) is diluted to the concentration of 1 M. The lichi leaves are collected from the campus of Shenzhen University and used after washing, drying and smashing.

2 g dry powdered lichi leaves and 100 mL DI water are sealed into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the brown-yellow mixture is centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane to remove the residual leaves and large particles. Subsequently, after rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying, dark brown LCDs in solid state are collected with a production yield of approximately 5%.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Brucker MFP-3D infinity, Germany) are introduced to observe the morphology of LCDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250, USA) are employed to gather structure information of LCDs. FTIR spectra ranging from 600 to 3600 cm−1 are collected by the infrared reflection method. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, Perkin Elmer Lambda 750, USA) and photoluminescence (PL, Hitachi F7000, Japan) spectra are used to record the spectra of LCDs in DI water and HCl solution. Zeta potential (Malvern Nano-ZS90, England) is performed to determine the surface charge of LCDs in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:label: 2.4Weight
ce:section.ce:para:
The Q235 carbon steel samples with a dimension of 15 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm are immersed in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of various concentrations of LCDs for 96 h, and are rapidly weighed three times after washing and drying at intervals of 24 h. Before the test, all the Q235 carbon steel specimens are weighted three times through a precision electronic scale with an accuracy of 0.0001 g. The weight loss tests are carried out in a 60 mL vessel placed at room temperature. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency (η) are calculated following the equation below:
Corrosion rate
=
Δ W
At
η
=
W
corr
-
W
corr
′
W
corr
× 100 %
where ΔW, A and t are the average weight loss of three-time weigh, the surface area, the immersion time for Q235 carbon steel samples, respectively;
W
corr
and
W
corr
'
correspond to the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations of LCDs, respectively.

Test solutions are prepared as follows: LCDs are directly added into 1 M HCl to form various HCl solutions with 25, 50, 100 and 200 mg/L concentration of LCDs, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) are conducted in a PARSTAT 4000 + electrochemical station with a classical three-electrode system (Q235 carbon steel with the exposure area of 1 cm2 as the working electrode (WE), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode (RE) and a platinum plate as the counter electrode (CE)). All tests are achieved at room temperature. Before test, open circuit potential (OCP) is supposed to reach a stable state. EIS are measured from 105 to 10−2 Hz with 10 mV amplitude, and PDP are executed through scanning the potential range within ±250 mV versus OCP with 1 mV s−1 rate. All the electrochemical experiments are performed three times to ensure the reproducibility. The EIS data are fitted and analyzed with ZSimDemo 3.30 software.

After the weight loss measurement, the specimens are taken out from the test solutions, and then washed and dried, finally stored in vacuum oven for morphology observation. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS) analyses are selected to observe the two-dimensional morphology and elemental compositions of specimen surface. The 3D morphologies and chemical states of specimen surfaces are obtained via a 3D scanning measuring instrument (Keyence VR-3000, Japan) and FTIR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA), respectively.

Hydrothermal approach is regarded to be the most ideal method to greenly synthesize CDs, achieving effortless preparation for highly water-soluble CDs without severe synthetic conditions, further surface passivation or noxious organic solvents/oxidants [36]. Meanwhile, natural lichi leaves with prominent merits of abundance, easy availability and eco-friendliness are the preferable biomass source. Hence, hydrothermal treatment is selected to synthesize biomass-derived CDs (i.e., LCDs) applying lichi leaves as the only raw material, which is convenient to operate and beneficial to macroscopic production. As presented in Fig. 1 , in a typical synthesis, 2 g dry powdered lichi leaves and 100 mL DI water are encapsulated into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After purification and drying, the LCDs with high water solubility are obtained.

The particle size distribution and microstructure of the as-prepared LCDs are directly observed by TEM. The majority of LCDs with a size distribution of 2.56–6.45 nm in range and ca. 4.24 nm on average by randomly measuring more than 100 particles, are well dispersed and present a quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 2
a). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals that most of LCDs own crystalline structures with well-resolved lattice fringes, and their lattice spacing of 0.21 nm well agrees with graphene (100) planes (Fig. 2 b) [37]. Topographic heights from the bulk of LCDs fall in the range of 0.5–1.5 nm indicated by the AFM image (Fig. 2 c and d), suggesting that LCDs have 1–3 graphene layers and are classified as graphene quantum dots (GQDs, one type of CDs) according to the lateral dimensions larger than their height [38]. All the conclusions from TEM and AFM firmly demonstrate that carbohydrates as major constituents of lichi leaves go through dehydration condensation, polymerization and further carbonization to form LCDs with sp2 C structure as the core under high temperature and high pressure.

Exhaustive information about surface chemical states and the elemental composition of the as-synthesized LCDs are uncovered by FTIR spectra and XPS. The characteristic peaks at 3333, 1721, 1444 and 1038 cm−1 in FTIR spectra (Fig. 3
a) confirm the presence of OH/NH, CO, CN, and CO bonds respectively [30,35]. Numerous O- and N-containing functional groups endow LCDs with excellent water solubility and considerable dispersible stability in aqueous systems, laying the foundation for their application as a water-based corrosion inhibitor. Besides, the peaks at 2920 and 2853 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibrations of LCDs, while the favorable hydrophilicity of LCDs implies a lower proportion of CH than that of O- and N-containing functional groups. The obvious peak at 1612 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of CC in benzene rings [34], demonstrating the sp2 C structure of LCDs, which is consistent with the results drawn from TEM. In the full scan XPS spectra, there are three typical peaks at 284, 532 and 399 eV, ascribed to C1s, O1s and N1s respectively ( Fig. S1 ). The relative atomic contents of LCDs are found to be 72.09% for C, 25.86% for O and 2.04% for N, attributed to the composition of lichi leaves (primarily consisting of carbohydrates) [39]. The C1s peaks at 284.4, 285.9 and 288.3 eV are related to CC/CC, CO/CN and CO, respectively. The CC/CC bond with a dominant content of 63.7% manifests that LCDs contain abundant sp2 C structure, showing satisfactory agreement with TEM studies. The O1s analysis reveals the presence of CO at 532.4 eV and CO at 531.4 eV, and the N1s spectrum displays two peaks at 399.6 and 401.7 eV assigned to CN and N(C)3, respectively [40]. Overall, the XPS data well coincide with the result of FTIR, intensively supporting the fact that LCDs are comprised of the sp2 C core and the passivated surface of O- and N-containing functional groups which contribute to corrosion inhibition actions. In addition, the Zeta potential of LCDs is −23.3 mV, proving their stability in aqueous solution.

The 1 mg/mL LCDs aqueous solution displays faint yellow color when exposed to visible light and emits a strong blue PL excited by a 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. S2
a) shows that LCDs has two absorption peaks at 275 and 320 nm ascribed to π − π* transition of aromatic sp2 C and n − π* transition of O- and N-containing functional groups [41], respectively. As illustrated in PL spectra ( Fig. S2
b), LCDs exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior with the excitation/emission maximum at 360/436 nm. The above optical properties afford a unique way to evaluate the long-term dispersion stability of LCDs in HCl solution.

Good dispersion stability of corrosion inhibitors in corrosive solution is crucial for their practical applications. Fig. S3 illustrates photographs of LCDs with different concentrations in 1 M HCl under daylight and UV light after standing for two weeks. It is dramatically evident that there is no visible sediment in these photographs, and with the concentration of LCDs increasing, the solution shows more noticeable yellow color under daylight. Meanwhile, it is well known that the PL of CDs will weaken or vanish if they suffer from agglomeration owing to their aggregation-induced quenching effect. As the content of LCDs rising, bule emitting PL stemming from the solution under the irradiation of a UV light gradually enhances, confirming that LCDs are well dispersive without agglomeration. Consequently, all the observations above qualitatively suggest distinguished long-term dispersion stability of LCDs in 1 M HCl. To quantitatively analyze their long-term dispersion stability, the UV–vis and PL spectra of LCDs with different concentrations in 1 M HCl are recorded. As exhibited in Fig. 4
a, regarding HCl solutions with different content of LCDs, the intensities of emission maximum peaks at around 390 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 285 nm noticeably depend on LCDs concentration, and enhance with increasing of LCDs content. Uniformly, the UV–vis absorption peaks at approximately 280 nm of solutions share the tendency of PL peaks (Fig. 4 b). Crucially, intensities of PL and UV–vis peaks for LCDs at concentrations of 25 and 50 mg/L almost unchanged after two weeks (Fig. 4 c and 4d). The results from quantitative analyses firmly indicate the excellent long-term dispersion stability of LCDs in 1 M HCl, precisely coinciding with qualitative observations. It is worth noting that with respect to those of LCDs aqueous solution, PL and UV–vis spectra of LCDs in HCl solution undergo apparent changes since H+-induced protonation of LCDs intensively affects their emission sites. Multitudes of literatures have found the similar phenomenon [42–44]. In a word, qualitative and quantitative analyses simultaneously manifest the long-term dispersion stability of LCDs in 1 M HCl, providing a solid grounding in LCDs application as an acid-based corrosion inhibitor.

Owing to its superior merits such as cost-effectiveness and high stress strength, carbon steel is one of the most extensively employed metals in engineering and daily life applications. In consideration of the case, the carbon steel is used as the experiment subject. To find out effects of LCDs as an inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl, the weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement are executed. Fig. S4 and Fig. 5
a depicts the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel and inhibition efficiency (η) of LCDs in 1 M HCl with different concentrations of LCDs under various immersion time at room temperature, respectively. The relatively detailed values are tabulated in Table S1. In sharp contrast to that of the blank solution, after adding LCDs, the corrosion rate greatly decreases regardless of LCDs concentration or immersion time. And with the growth of LCDs concentration, the corrosion rate shows a significant downtrend. When the content of LCDs is 200 mg/L, the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel declines by 86.25% (from 2.40 to 0.33 g∙cm−2∙h−1) after immersion of 24 h with respect to that of blank solution ( Fig. S4 ). More importantly, with the extension of immersion (from 24 to 96 h), the corrosion rate possesses a reduction from 86.25% to 93.02% ( Fig. S4 and Table S1), suggesting the eminent potential for LCDs as a long-term corrosion inhibitor. Correspondingly, η values are dramatically improved after adding LCDs (Fig. 5 a). It can be observed that the η value reaches the highest value of 86.10% at 200 mg/L of LCDs after immersion of 24 h, and increases to 92.73% with prolonged immersion to 96 h ( Fig. S4 and Table S1). All the conclusions from the weight loss test clearly demonstrate highly efficient inhibition performance and promising long-term inhibition potential of LCDs.

To further evaluate inhibition properties of LCDs, EIS and PDP tests of Q235 carbon steel as the electrode in 1 M HCl with different concentrations of LCDs at room temperature are measured. As displayed in Fig. S5 , with the incorporation of LCDs, the open circuit potential (OCP) values of the Q235 carbon steel electrode move to negative direction in comparison with that of blank HCl solution, implying that LCDs hinder the precipitation and discharge of hydrogen in the cathode region of carbon steel surface, thereby increasing the overpotential of the hydrogen evolution reaction process and suppressing the cathodic corrosion process [2]. EIS is applied to investigate the behavior of the carbon steel/solution interface in the absence and presence of LCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 b, the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (0.01 Hz) is inversely proportional to corrosion rate of carbon steel electrode [42,44], revealing a rising trend as the concentration of LCDs increases, thereby symbolizing a gradually enhanced corrosion protection performance on the surface of Q235 carbon steel. Significantly, the |Z|0.01 Hz value attains 403 Ω cm2 in HCl solution with 200 mg/L LCDs, boosting 95.13% than that of blank solution. Furthermore, regardless of the concentration of LCDs, all Bode-phase angle curves illustrate a single peak implying one time constant. But their peaks advance with the increasing content of LCDs, uncovering a stronger capacitive response at carbon steel/solution interface, which is ascribed to the adsorption of LCDs [45]. As described in Fig. 5 c, depressed capacitive semicircles are observed from the representative Nyquist plots, their diameters markedly enlarge with the incorporation of LCDs, manifesting that LCDs are able to form an efficacious protective film on the surface of carbon steel [46] even at lower concentration of 25 mg/L, and the compactness of the protective film is improved with increase of LCDs concentration. Moreover, all capacitive semicircles display the similar shapes with and without LCDs, suggesting that LCDs fail to alter the electrochemical characteristics of Q235 carbon steel electrode [17], in line with the finding of Bode plots.

To better comprehend EIS results, the equivalent circuit model (Fig. 5 c) is employed, and relevant parameter values are listed in Table 1 . Among them, Rs is the solution resistance between the working and reference electrodes. Rp corresponds to the polarization resistance, which can be used to calculate the corrosion inhibition efficiency (γ): γ = (Rp − Rp
0)/Rp × 100% [47], where Rp and Rp
0 represent the polarization resistances with and without corrosion inhibitors, respectively. CPE dl is the constant phase element representing the double-layer capacitance instead of an ideal capacitor, ascribed to the non-ideal capacitive behavior of the inhomogeneous electrode [48]. CPE dl is related to the corrosion reaction area [49], calculated by the equation: CPE dl = Y 0(2πf max)n−1, where Y 0 is the magnitude of the CPE dl ; fmax represents frequency at the maximum value of impedance imaginary component; The parameter n (−1 < n < 1) offers information about the degree of working electrode surface inhomogeneity, and the CPE is equal to an ideal capacitor when n = 1 [50]. As listed in Table 1, with the addition of LCDs, Rp values increase, meanwhile CPE dl values decrease, implying that LCDs as an efficacious corrosion inhibitor are supposed to adsorb the surface of Q235 carbon steel electrode, then reduce the corrosion area and provide an eminent protection action. These positive effects are significantly strengthened with the increasing content of LCDs. Specifically, the γ value reaches a maximum of 95.13% at 200 mg/L LCDs solution within the existing concentration range, meaning that LCDs are able to offer an effective protection for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl.

PDP technique is performed to further monitor the process and mechanism of the anodic and cathodic reactions, and evaluate the inhibitor effect of LCDs on these reactions. Fig. 5 d depicts PDP curves of Q235 carbon steel electrode in various test solutions, and carrying out classical extrapolation to calculate the Tafel part of PDP curve. The correlation parameters are summarized in Table 2 including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathode slope (β c), anode slope (β a) and inhibition efficiency (IE). Among them, IE value is recorded as the following formula: IE = (i 0 corr − i corr)/i 0 corr × 100%, where i 0 corr and i corr are corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, respectively. Combined with Fig. 5 d and Table 2, both cathodic and anodic regions shift to lower i corr with LCDs concentration increasing, and both β c and β a share the similar tendency towards smaller values, indicating that LCDs, as mixed type inhibitor, have an overt inhibiting effect on both cathodic and anodic reactions. Meanwhile, β a values decrease more sharply than β c values, suggesting that the anodic reaction is principally restrained. But the E corr absolute shift is less than 85 mV, thus LCDs show mixed-type inhibitive actions, while prominently inhibit the reaction on the anode [51]. It is worth mentioning that i corr values remarkably decrease with a downtrend as LCDs concentration increases, illustrating that LCDs efficiently reduce corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel. Correspondingly, IE values reflecting the inhibition capacity dramatically increase in the presence of LCDs, and achieve maximum of 98.06% in 200 mg/L LCDs solution. All the consequences above show a close agreement with weight loss test and EIS analyses.

All the results from the weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement certify that biomass-derived LCDs possess highly efficient inhibition performance and the promising long-term inhibition potential for Q235 carbon steel in the acid corrosive solution, potentially attributed to the efficacious protective film formed by adsorption of LCDs on the surface of Q235 carbon steel. The relevant inhibition mechanism will be detailedly investigated below.

The adsorption isotherm exhibited in Fig. S6 can supply critical information about interaction between the LCDs and Q235 carbon steel surface to probe into corrosion inhibition mechanism. According to the Equation θ/(1 − θ) = K ads∙C inh (θ means the surface coverage shown in Table 1; K ads and C inh are adsorption equilibrium constant and inhibitor concentration respectively), the EIS results yield the best fit of Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, presenting an ideal linear correlation with R2 of 0.9989 ( Fig. S6 ). Consequently, the adsorption process of LCDs inhibitor precisely conforms to Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Additionally, the adsorption free energy (ΔG 0 ads) is determined through Equation ΔG 0 ads = −RTIn(1000K ads) (T and R are the thermodynamic temperature of 298 K and the molar gas constant of 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, respectively), employed to evaluate the adsorption type of corrosion inhibitors. If the ΔG 0 ads value is −20 kJ−1 mol−1 or more positive, there will an electrostatic interaction between inhibitor molecules and metal surface, called as physical adsorption; While if the ΔG 0 ads value is −40 kJ−1 mol−1 or more negative, coordinate covalent bond will be generated induced by transfer or sharing of electrons from inhibitor molecules to metal surface, named as chemical adsorption [52,53]. The ΔG 0 ads value of LCDs is −27.43 kJ−1 mol−1, suggesting that the interaction between LCDs and Q235 carbon steel surface involves both chemisorption and physisorption.

For a better understanding of inhibition mechanism of LCDs, corrosion morphologies of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl without and with 200 mg/L LCDs for 96 h, are comprehensively analyzed by 3D scanning measuring instrument, SEM, EDS and FTIR. 3D morphologies ( Fig. S7 ) distinctly present that the surface of Q235 carbon steel after immersion in blank HCl is seriously corroded, producing a great number of large pits resulting from aggressive acid attack, and its surface roughness (S a) value is 12.428 μm; By comparison, after adding LCDs, the surface of Q235 carbon steel becomes smoother with unobtrusively shallow pits, and the S a value starkly reduces to 3.264 μm (73.7% drop) which is close to the value (1.705 μm) of original Q235 carbon steel. As a result, LCDs effectively suppress the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl, further verified by SEM images. As presented in Fig. 6 , the surface of original Q235 carbon steel is highly smooth with a few faint scratches and negligible pits, while after 96 h immersion in 1 M HCl, the Q235 carbon steel surface shows severe damage with obviously large pits and small cracks. With the addition of 200 mg/L LCDs, the corrosion is significantly alleviated, leading to disappearance of small cracks and large pits, which is possibly ascribed to the formation of LCDs adsorption film on the steel surface. To validate the hypothesis, corresponding surface elemental compositions and chemical states are measured by EDS and FTIR, respectively. As uncovered in Fig. 6 and Table S2, all the Q235 carbon steel samples mainly consist of C, O and Fe elements. Compared with those of original Q235 carbon steel, Fe atom content of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl observably decreases from 69.50% to 42.62%, while its O atom content sharply increases from 3.80% to 39.72%, assigned to the formation of a great number of corrosion products on the metal surface [54]. Regarding Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl with 200 mg/L LCDs, atomic contents of Fe and C are 55.33% and 24.9% respectively, owing an increase in comparison with those of Q235 carbon steel in blank HCl solution, thus LCDs accumulate on Q235 carbon steel surface to form a protective film, inhibiting steel dissolution inflicted by HCl. It should be pointed out that resulting from low N atom content (2.04% recorded by XPS) of LCDs, it is hard for EDS to availably detect it. In addition, as revealed by FTIR ( Fig. S8 ), LCDs and the Q235 carbon steel surface exposed to 1 M HCl with 200 mg/L LCDs share multiple same peaks such as CH, OH/NH, CN, CO and CO. Meanwhile, these peaks tend to slightly shift with great changes on their intensities, and the CC peak of LCDs cannot be discovered on carbon steel surface, attributed to formation of coordination bonds or/and other interactions [29] between LCDs and carbon steel surface, thereby further affording persuasive evidence for the formation of LCDs-derived protective film.

Based on all the analyses above, a rational model is proposed to illustrate the inhibition mechanism of LCDs (Fig. 7 ). As for Q235 carbon steel in HCl without LCDs, the anodic reaction is Fe − 2e− = Fe2+, giving rise to rapid dissolution of carbon steel. At the same time, the cathodic reaction is dominated by 2H+ + 2e− = H2↑, and Cl ions also play a depolarizing role to accelerate electrochemical corrosion process of carbon steel. As a consequence, the carbon steel surface is seriously corroded. After adding LCDs, benefiting from their abundant O- and N-containing functional groups confirmed by FTIR and XPS (Fig. 3), LCDs easily adsorb on carbon steel substrate by coordination bonds formed between lone pair electrons of O/N atom and the unoccupied 3d orbital of Fe atoms. According to adsorption isotherm investigation, apart from chemical adsorption, physical adsorption (mainly electrostatic interaction) also contributes to the establishment of the LCDs-induced absorption film. In detail, with the addition of LCDs, they are immediately protonated by H+, leading to be positively charged. Then the negatively charged hydrated Cl ions attaching onto the steel surface act as the interconnecting bridge [25] to advance the physical adsorption of protonated LCDs on the mental surface through electrostatic interaction, thereby occasioning a competitive relation with H+. Sequentially, a protective film created by LCDs chemical and physical adsorptions is successfully formed on carbon steel substrate, testified by EDS and FTIR. With the assistance of the film, the direct contact between corrosive medium and mental can be remarkably reduced, thereby simultaneously retarding the metal dissolution process at anode and hydrogen evolution process at cathode, which is certified by PDP. It is worth mentioning that with the LCDs content increasing, the adsorption film accomplishes a better cover on carbon steel surface, further significantly inhibiting corrosion, firmly proved by EIS data. Furthermore, LCDs are able to block the active sites on carbon steel surface and further suppress the corrosion [55]. Additionally, the small size of LCDs is conductive to promote the compactness of adsorption film. To sum up, the protective film formed by LCDs chemical and physical adsorptions on the metal substrate is perceived as their inhibition mechanism.

In summary, this work firstly demonstrates the outstanding corrosion inhibition capacities of green biomass-derived CDs (i.e., LCDs), and unveils their inhibition mechanism. Specifically, LCDs are prepared by green, facile and one-step hydrothermal processing using lichi leaves as the raw material, avoiding the employment of hazardous elements and the generation of toxic gases. The as-obtained eco-friendly LCDs with sp2 C core and the passivated surface of O- and N-containing functional groups present a long-term dispersion stability in HCl solution, laying a solid foundation for their application as an acid-based corrosion inhibitor. Applying weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement, corrosion inhibition behaviors of LCDs for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl are systematically investigated, illustrating that LCDs with 200 mg/L concentration possess an outstanding inhibition rate up to 98.06%, thereby serving as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in acid solution. In addition, their inhibition mechanism studied by adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology analyses is ascribed to the protective film generated by LCDs chemical and physical adsorptions. Our findings contribute to raise awareness about the amazing corrosion inhibition properties of biomass-derived CDs and open up a new way for preparation of green and efficient corrosion inhibitors. It should be noted that the production yield of CDs using the leaves as the raw material can be up to 30% by the hydrothermal method [35]. Hence, the production yield of LCDs is expected to promoted by the optimization of the preparation parameters, which would be the focus of our next research.
Wu-Jian Long: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xue-Qi Li: Software, Writing – review & editing. Yang Yu: Investigation, Data curation. Chuang He: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundations of China, NSFC-Shandong Joint Fund (No. U2006223), the Science and Technology Project of Shenzhen, China (No. JCYJ20190808151011502), the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Marine Civil Engineering (SZU) (No. 2020B1212060074) and the Guangdong University Student Science and Technology Innovation Cultivation Special Fund Project (No. pdjh2021b0428). Furthermore, the authors would also like to thank Tang Xiang Gang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for SEM and EDS measurement. In addition, the authors thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University for the assistance with analysis.
